one_line_takeaway: Ten harmless DPO preference pairs that merely prefer helpful answers over refusals can broadly suppress a fine-tuned LLM's refusal behavior and jailbreak it (up to 81.73% ASR on GPT-4.1-nano for $0.10), while remaining undetectable to both content moderation and frontier-LLM intent auditors.
key_finding: On HEx-PHI the attack achieves the highest ASR among benign fine-tuning attacks: 59.13% (GPT-4o), 70.20% (GPT-4.1), 54.80% (GPT-4.1-mini), and 81.73% (GPT-4.1-nano), at fine-tuning costs of only $1.7, $1.7, $0.3, and $0.1 respectively, corresponding to 4.96x/10.64x/5.59x/13.78x increases over the base models. Crucially, it also evades stronger auditing: the OpenAI Moderation API flags 0% of every method, and frontier LLM auditors (GPT-5-mini, GPT-5.5, DeepSeek V4 Pro, Gemini 3.1 Pro) flag this attack at 0.00% (Claude Opus 4.7 at only 8.00%) while flagging AOA/NOICE/TenBenign at ~62-100%. The mechanistic analysis shows why: preferred-response cross-entropy stays low while the CE loss on refusals to UNSEEN harmful queries rises in lockstep with the dispreferred-refusal loss, and the mean DPO gradient is positively aligned with answering harmful prompts (0.1580) and negatively with refusing (-0.1351), versus near-orthogonal SFT gradients (0.0723 / 0.0090). Even a single benign preference pair suffices on open-weight models.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the 'Truly Benign DPO Attack,' a fine-tuning attack that weakens the safety alignment of frontier LLMs using only 10 harmless Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) preference pairs (the minimum accepted by OpenAI's fine-tuning API). Each pair contains a benign prompt, the base model's own helpful answer as the PREFERRED response, and a standard refusal as the DISPREFERRED response. The authors' central claim is that unlike prior benign fine-tuning attacks, this data is benign in both content AND apparent intent: it is essentially indistinguishable from a legitimate over-refusal-reduction request, so harmful intent cannot be inferred from the request alone. Because DPO optimizes a relative preference margin (pushing the model away from refusals rather than imitating a new behavior), suppressing refusals on benign prompts transfers to unseen harmful prompts. This is the paper's OWN contribution; the four prior attacks (Indirect, AOA, TenBenign, NOICE) are described as background/baselines, not as this paper's method.
threat_model: Fine-tuning-as-a-service, black-box. The attacker has no access to model weights, training infrastructure, or internal safety mechanisms and must submit a fine-tuning job through the same official API as ordinary users, subject to the provider's moderation checks, usage constraints, and token-based pricing (e.g., GPT-4o fine-tuning at $25 per million training tokens). The goal is to weaken the fine-tuned model's safety alignment while the submitted data looks like an ordinary customization request. The paper defines four attack criteria: (1) benign content (no harmful prompts/completions, covert encodings, or adversarial formatting); (2) benign apparent intent (consistent with a legitimate user objective such as reducing over-refusal); (3) minimal data and low cost; (4) selective safety weakening that preserves downstream utility.
limitations: The authors' stated limitations (Appendix G): the evaluation targets currently available fine-tuning pipelines and future safety-training methods may change how the failure mode manifests; the study covers DPO-style preference optimization only, and other preference-learning objectives may behave differently; deployed services may include additional safeguards (human review, post-training safety checks) not modeled here, so results should be read as evidence of an important failure mode rather than a complete characterization. Reviewer-noted caveats: harmfulness judgment relies on an LLM judge (GPT-5-mini), though it is human-validated; a real fine-tuning provider could combine the disclosed behavioral-audit and inference-time defenses (e.g., Aligned Model Defense, output filters), which the paper acknowledges partially mitigate but do not eliminate the underlying weakened alignment; effectiveness of the OpenAI-model attacks depends on the current API accepting DPO jobs of this shape (TenBenign jobs were in fact blocked on the GPT-4.1 family).

datasets_used: HEx-PHI (300 harmful instructions, 10 prohibited categories) - primary harmfulness benchmark
TenBenign (10 benign prompts) - shared source for constructing the attack and all baselines
XSTest (250 safe prompts) - realistic over-refusal-reduction scenario
HarmBench, SorryBench, StrongREJECT, JailbreakBench - four additional jailbreak benchmarks
GSM8k, IFEval, GPQA Diamond - downstream utility benchmarks
quantitative_results: HEx-PHI ASR (Ours): GPT-4o 59.13%, GPT-4.1 70.20%, GPT-4.1-mini 54.80%, GPT-4.1-nano 81.73%

Dispreferred-response ablation on GPT-4o (Table 1): default refusal 59.13%, 'I can't fulfill that request.' 57.60%, 'I apologize, but I cannot fulfill your request.' 60.13%, 10 refusal variants 68.40%, non-refusal control 21.00%
Preferred-response ablation (Table 2): Ours vs Vanilla-Aligned = 59.13% vs 52.73% (GPT-4o), 70.20% vs 65.33% (GPT-4.1)
LoRA vs full fine-tuning on Llama-3.1 8B (Table 3, Ours): LoRA 84.56% vs Full 63.33%
XSTest over-refusal scenario on Llama-3.1 8B (Table 4): over-refusal rate 8.80% -> 0.40%, ASR 11.33% -> 87.33%
Gradient cosine similarity: DPO gradient aligned with answering +0.1580 and with refusal -0.1351; SFT/Indirect gradient +0.0723 (answer) and +0.0090 (refusal)
Detectability (Table 5): OpenAI Moderation API 0.00% for all; frontier auditors flag Ours at 0.00% (Claude Opus 4.7 8.00%) vs AOA ~100%, NOICE 98-100%, TenBenign 62-100%
Judge validation (Table 7): GPT-5-mini vs Human1 82.3%, vs Human2 81.2%, Human1 vs Human2 76.0%
Downstream GSM8k (Table 6, Ours): GPT-4o 92.19, GPT-4.1 80.44, GPT-4.1-mini 86.35, GPT-4.1-nano 76.72 (vs base 86.28/79.98/85.29/82.49)
baselines_compared: Indirect Attack (Li et al. 2024) - compliant-prefix SFT
AOA / Absolutely Obedient Agent Attack (Qi et al. 2024) - identity-shifting persona
TenBenign Attack (Xie et al. 2025) - two-stage overfitting on 10 benign QA pairs
NOICE Attack (Kazdan et al. 2026) - refuse-then-comply pattern
ReNeLLM (Ding et al. 2024) - prompt-based jailbreak, in Appendix E comparison
